title: 2026 Ethiopian Harvest Forecast: What Global Roasters Need to Know
excerpt: Analyzing the upcoming Yirgacheffe and Sidama micro-crops, expected volume constraints, and early price differential projections for the 2026 export season.
content: As we approach the 2026 harvest window, early agronomy reports from our partner washing stations in Guji and Sidama indicate a highly concentrated, dense crop. Prolonged rains during the late maturation phase have extended the growing cycle, which typically results in exceptional cup complexity and higher density green coffee. However, volume across grade 1 and grade 2 washed lots is projected to be slightly below historical averages. Global roasters relying on specific Ethiopian flavor profiles are advised to secure early forward contracts to guarantee allocation before the main harvest is completed.
author: Lot 251 Trade Desk

title: Understanding Ethiopian Coffee Grades: G1 Through G5
excerpt: A technical breakdown of physical preparation standards, secondary defect tolerances, and what an export-grade Lot 251 bag guarantees.
content: Ethiopian coffee grading can often be confusing for international buyers. The grading system (G1 through G5) is determined entirely by physical defect counts and cup cleanliness, not inherently by flavor potential. For specialty export, Lot 251 exclusively deals in Grade 1 (0-3 defects per 300g) and Grade 2 (4-12 defects) for washed coffees. Furthermore, our internal milling specifications require an aggressive screen size separation (minimum 14/15) and a strict 10-11% moisture boundary. Understanding these metrics ensures that when you specify a contract with us, the physical preparation matches the cupping score.
author: Yohannes Girma

title: Direct Trade vs. ECX: How Lot 251 Sources Outside the Auction
excerpt: Why identity preservation matters, and how bypassing the central commodity exchange ensures higher payout for washing stations.
content: Historically, all Ethiopian export coffee passed through the Ethiopia Commodity Exchange (ECX), a system built to standardize the national crop but which inherently stripped coffees of their micro-regional identity. Under updated export regulations, Lot 251 operates a direct, vertically integrated supply chain. By contracting directly with washing stations in Yirgacheffe and Harar, we preserve the exact farm-level provenance of every 60kg bag. This not only allows roasters to trace their coffee back to specific hillsides, but the removal of middle-tier bureaucracy allows us to return a 15-20% higher premium directly to the producing washing stations.
author: Lot 251 Trade Desk

title: Water Activity & Shelf Life: A Buyer's Guide to Green Coffee Storage
excerpt: Exploring Aw (Water Activity) metrics in Ethiopian Naturals and how our Addis Ababa warehouse climate control stabilizes export lots.
content: While moisture percentage is critical, Water Activity (Aw) is the true indicator of green coffee stability and the prevention of microbial growth during maritime transit. Ethiopian naturals, particularly those from Guji, require extended, highly supervised drying phases to stabilize Aw below 0.60. At our Addis Ababa export terminal, every incoming lot is tested for both moisture and Aw. Coffees are then rested in climate-monitored zones before stuffing into grain-pro lined containers. For international roasters, understanding this metric is crucial to predicting how a coffee will age and perform 6 to 9 months post-harvest.
author: Yohannes Girma
